segments.inferno-3-g1: “Per me” means “through me,” with the gate speaking as the means of entry. “Si va” is an impersonal construction, literally “one goes,” and “ne la” is the older separated spelling of modern “nella,” here indicating motion into “la città dolente.”
segments.inferno-3-g2: This repeats the same inscriptional formula: “per me si va” means “through me one goes.” “Ne l’etterno dolore” is an archaic separated form of “nell’etterno dolore,” with “etterno” as a poetic spelling of “eterno.”
segments.inferno-3-g3: The third parallel clause again uses impersonal “si va.” “Tra la perduta gente” means “among the lost/damned people,” with “perduta” agreeing in feminine singular with “gente,” a collective singular noun.
segments.inferno-3-g4: “Giustizia” is the subject, personified as the divine justice that impelled the Creator to make this gate. “Mosse” is a past-tense form of “muovere,” “moved,” and “il mio alto fattore” is the object, meaning “my lofty Maker/Creator.”
segments.inferno-3-g5: “Fecemi” is an archaic contraction of “fece mi,” meaning “made me,” with the object pronoun attached to the verb. The subject begins after the verb: “la divina podestate,” that is, “divine power.”
segments.inferno-3-g6: This segment completes the compound subject of “fecemi”: divine power, supreme wisdom, and primal love made the gate. “’l” is the elided form of “il,” and “primo amore” refers to the first or primal divine love.
segments.inferno-3-g7: “Dinanzi a me” means “before me” in time, not in physical position. “Fuor” is an archaic poetic form of “furono,” “were,” and the phrase “se non” means “except”; thus the clause says that no created things existed before this gate except eternal ones.
segments.inferno-3-g8: “Io” is the speaking gate, continuing the first-person voice of the inscription. “Duro” is not the adjective “hard” here but a verb from “durare,” meaning “I last/endure,” while “etterno” functions adverbially or predicatively: “eternally” or “eternal.”
segments.inferno-3-g9: “Lasciate” is a second-person plural imperative: the inscription directly commands those entering. “Ogne” is an archaic form of “ogni,” and “ogne speranza” means “every hope” or “all hope.”
segments.inferno-3-g10: “Voi” addresses the entrants in the plural. “Ch’intrate” is “che intrate,” where “ch’” is the relative pronoun “who” and “intrate” is an archaic form of “entrate,” “you enter.”
segments.inferno-3-g11: The object “Queste parole di colore oscuro” is placed before the verb for emphasis: literally, “these words of dark color.” “vid’ ïo” is poetic for “vidi io,” with elision and a marked pronoun; “scritte” is a feminine plural past participle agreeing with “parole,” and “al sommo d’una porta” means “at/on the top of a gate.”
segments.inferno-3-g12: “per ch’io” is an abbreviated causal/result expression, equivalent to “per che io,” meaning “whereupon/for which reason I...” The verb of speaking is omitted after “io” and supplied by the following direct quotation.
segments.inferno-3-g13: “Maestro” is a vocative, directly addressing Virgil as Dante’s guide and teacher. The noun stands alone before the rest of Dante’s statement.
segments.inferno-3-g14: “lor” is an archaic possessive meaning “their,” referring back to the written words. “m’è duro” contracts “mi è duro”: literally “is hard to me,” meaning that the meaning is difficult for Dante to understand or bear.
segments.inferno-3-g15: “Ed elli a me” means “And he to me,” with the verb of speaking understood from context. “elli” is an archaic form of “egli,” “he,” referring to Virgil, though the supplied dictionary senses do not match this use.
segments.inferno-3-g16: “come persona accorta” modifies Virgil’s manner of speaking: he replies “as a prudent/clear-sighted person.” “Accorta” is feminine singular because it agrees with “persona,” even though the person referred to is male.
segments.inferno-3-g17: “Qui” marks the threshold they have reached: “here.” “si convien lasciare” is an impersonal construction meaning “one must leave behind”; “convien” is an archaic/poetic form of “conviene,” and “ogne” is an old spelling of “ogni.”
segments.inferno-3-g18: The subject is “ogne viltà,” “every cowardice,” placed before the verb for emphasis. “convien che ... sia morta” means “it is necessary that ... be dead,” with “sia” in the subjunctive after the impersonal necessity expression “convien che.”
segments.inferno-3-g19: “Noi siam venuti” is a compound perfect with “essere,” here “we have come/arrived.” “ov’ i’ t’ho detto” means “where I told you,” with elisions of “ove,” “io,” and “ti”; the following “che tu vedrai...” gives the content of what Virgil had said. “c’hanno” is “che hanno,” a relative clause referring to “le genti dolorose,” and “il ben de l’intelletto” means the good proper to intellect or understanding, traditionally the lost highest good.
segments.inferno-3-g20: “E poi che” introduces a temporal subordinate clause: “And after.” “puose” is an archaic preterite form of “pose/put,” and the poetic order “la sua mano a la mia puose” means “he placed his hand to/in mine”; “con lieto volto” describes his reassuring expression.
segments.inferno-3-g21: “ond’” is elided from “onde” and means “from which/whereby,” linking Dante’s renewed courage to Virgil’s cheerful gesture. “mi confortai” is reflexive in form: literally “I comforted myself,” idiomatically “I took heart.”
segments.inferno-3-g22: “mi mise” literally means “he put me,” but in context it means “he led/brought me.” “dentro a le” is the older separated form corresponding to modern “dentro alle,” and “le segrete cose” refers to the hidden or secret matters/realms into which Dante is now being brought.
segments.inferno-3-g47: The conjunction “E” links Dante’s question to the preceding speech, and “io” is the explicit subject of an implied verb of saying, as in “and I said.” This elliptical reporting formula is common in dialogue.
segments.inferno-3-g48: “Maestro” is a vocative, directly addressing Virgil. It stands outside the syntax of the question that follows.
segments.inferno-3-g49: The first “che” is interrogative, meaning “what,” with “è” as the present tense of “essere”: literally, “what is so burdensome to them.” “A lor” is an archaic/poetic dative form meaning “to them,” and the second “che” introduces a result clause after “tanto”: “so burdensome that...” In “lamentar li fa,” the infinitive “lamentar” depends on causative “fa,” while “li” is an object pronoun meaning “them,” placed before the verb: “makes them lament”; “sì forte” means “so loudly.”
segments.inferno-3-g50: This segment contains only punctuation and carries no independent grammatical content.
segments.inferno-3-g51: “Rispuose” is an archaic form of “rispose,” the past tense meaning “he replied.” The subject is understood from context, namely Virgil.
segments.inferno-3-g52: “Dicerolti” is a contracted poetic form combining the future verb “dirò/dicerò” with enclitic pronouns: “I will tell it to you.” “Molto breve” functions adverbially, literally “very brief,” meaning “very briefly” or “in few words.”
segments.inferno-3-g53: “Questi” means “these people,” referring to the souls just discussed. “Non hanno speranza di morte” uses the present tense “hanno” with a partitive-like complement introduced by “di”: they do not have hope of death, meaning even death is unavailable as a release.
segments.inferno-3-g54: “La lor cieca vita” means “their blind life,” with “lor” as an archaic possessive equivalent to “loro.” “È tanto bassa” sets up a consecutive construction: their existence is “so low” or abject that a result will follow in the next segment.
segments.inferno-3-g55: “Che” completes the “tanto...che” result construction: “so low that...” “’Nvidïosi” is an elided poetic form of “invidiosi,” and “son” is the shortened form of “sono,” “they are.” “D’ogne altra sorte” means “of every other fate/lot,” with “d’” contracted from “di” before a vowel.
segments.inferno-3-g39: This segment contains only sentence-ending punctuation; there are no words to parse or translate here.
segments.inferno-3-g40: “Ed” is the conjunction “and,” linking Virgil’s answer to the preceding question. “elli” is an archaic form of “egli,” meaning “he,” and “a me” means “to me”; the verb of saying is understood from the dialogue structure: “and he [said] to me.”
segments.inferno-3-g41: The object phrase “Questo misero modo” comes before the verb for emphasis: literally, “this wretched manner.” “tegnon” is an archaic shortened form of “tengono,” “they hold/keep,” with “l’anime triste” as its subject after the verb; “di coloro che...” specifies whose souls they are. “visser” is an apocopated preterite form of “vissero,” “they lived,” and “sanza” is an archaic spelling of “senza,” repeated in the balanced phrase “without infamy and without praise.”
segments.inferno-3-g42: “Mischiate sono” is an inverted passive/adjectival construction: “they are mingled,” referring back to “l’anime.” “a quel cattivo coro” means they are joined to or mixed with that base/wretched company; “de li” is the older separated form of modern “dei,” “of the.” The relative clause “che non furon ribelli né fur fedeli a Dio” uses shortened preterite forms “furon/fur” from “essere,” saying that the angels were neither rebels nor faithful to God.
segments.inferno-3-g43: “ma” contrasts this third position with the two rejected alternatives, rebellion and fidelity. “per sé” literally means “for themselves,” expressing self-interest or neutrality, and “fuoro” is an archaic form of “furono,” “they were.”
segments.inferno-3-g44: “Caccianli” combines the verb “cacciano” with the object pronoun “li”: “they drive them out.” The subject “i ciel” follows the verb, a common poetic inversion, and “per non esser men belli” is a purpose clause with “per” plus infinitive: “so as not to be less beautiful.”
segments.inferno-3-g45: “né” continues the negative coordination: not only do the heavens reject them, but Hell does not receive them either. “lo profondo inferno” has the adjective before the noun in a solemn poetic phrase, and “li” is a direct-object pronoun meaning “them,” not the article “the.”
segments.inferno-3-g46: “ch’” here means “because/for,” giving the reason Hell will not accept them. “i rei” means “the guilty” or “the damned,” and “avrebber” is a shortened conditional form of “avrebbero,” “would have”; “d’elli” means “from them” or “because of them,” referring to the rejected souls as a possible source of comparative glory for the damned.
segments.inferno-3-g23: “Quivi” is an archaic/poetic adverb meaning “there,” referring to the place Dante has just entered. “Sospiri” begins a plural subject list whose main verb comes later in “risonavan.”
segments.inferno-3-g24: “Pianti e alti guai” continues the plural subject list: “weeping and loud/high wails.” “Risonavan” is a poetic shortened form of “risonavano,” imperfect indicative, describing an ongoing sound that “resounded” through “l’aere sanza stelle,” literally “the air without stars.”
segments.inferno-3-g25: “Per ch’io” is the contracted form of “per che io,” here meaning “wherefore/so that I,” linking Dante’s weeping as the result of the sounds. “Al cominciar” uses a nominalized infinitive, “at the beginning,” and “lagrimai” is an archaic spelling of “lacrimai,” “I wept”; “ne” means “because of it/them” or “at it.”
segments.inferno-3-g26: “Diverse lingue” is the first item in a long compound subject whose verb is delayed until “facevano.” “Lingue” can mean both literal “tongues” and “languages,” fitting the idea of confused human cries.
segments.inferno-3-g27: “Orribili favelle” adds another plural noun phrase to the list. “Favelle” is a literary plural meaning forms of speech or utterances, modified by “orribili.”
segments.inferno-3-g28: “Parole di dolore” is another appositional item in the same list of sounds. The preposition “di” expresses quality or content: the words are characterized by pain or sorrow.
segments.inferno-3-g29: “Accenti d’ira” continues the catalogue, with “accenti” meaning uttered tones or cries rather than merely stress marks. “D’ira” is elided from “di ira,” meaning “of anger.”
segments.inferno-3-g30: “Voci alte e fioche” adds contrasting kinds of voices, “loud/high” and “faint.” The adjectives are feminine plural to agree with “voci.”
segments.inferno-3-g31: “E suon di man con elle” adds “the sound of hands along with them,” where “elle” is a feminine plural pronoun referring back to the voices or sounds. “Facevano” is imperfect indicative and finally supplies the main verb for the whole long subject list: all these sounds “were making” or “made” one uproar.
segments.inferno-3-g32: “Il qual” is a relative pronoun, “which,” referring back to “tumulto.” “S’aggira” is pronominal/reflexive in form and means “goes whirling/circling”; “in quell’aura sanza tempo tinta” describes the air as darkly stained and outside ordinary time.
segments.inferno-3-g33: “Come” introduces a simile comparing the circling uproar to sand stirred by wind. “Quando turbo spira” means “when a whirlwind blows”; here “spira” is a verb from “spirare,” not the noun “spiral/coil.”
segments.inferno-3-g34: “E io” resumes Dante as speaker, and “ch’avea” is “che avea,” with “avea” a poetic form of “aveva,” imperfect of “avere.” “D’error la testa cinta” has poetic word order: literally “had the head encircled with error/confusion,” meaning his mind was wrapped in bewilderment.
segments.inferno-3-g35: “Dissi” is the first-person singular passato remoto of “dire,” the standard narrative past tense in Dante. It introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-3-g36: “Maestro” is a vocative addressed to Virgil. It functions as “Master” or “Teacher,” marking Virgil’s role as Dante’s guide and instructor.
segments.inferno-3-g37: “Che è” asks “what is,” with “che” used interrogatively. “Quel ch’i’ odo” is “quello che io odo,” contracted poetically to mean “that which I hear.”
segments.inferno-3-g38: The initial “e” continues Dante’s direct question from the preceding sentence. “Che gent’ è” means “what people is this,” with “gent’” elided before the vowel; “che par nel duol sì vinta” is a relative clause, “that seems so overcome in grief,” where “duol” is a noun meaning grief or sorrow.
segments.inferno-3-g147: The subject is “La terra lagrimosa,” with the adjective following the noun in normal/poetic Italian: literally “the tearful earth.” “Diede” is passato remoto of “dare,” used narratively; “diede vento” means “gave forth/yielded a wind,” not simply handed something over.
segments.inferno-3-g148: “Che” links this clause to the preceding event, with the sense “whereupon/so that”; the subject of “balenò” is the postposed “una luce vermiglia,” so the order is poetic: “a red light flashed.” “La qual” is a relative pronoun referring to “luce,” and “mi” functions as a personal/possessive dative, meaning that the light overcame all of Dante’s sensory awareness.
segments.inferno-3-g149: “E caddi” continues the narrative with “caddi,” the first-person passato remoto: “and I fell.” The comparison “come l’uom cui sonno piglia” is archaic/poetic: “cui” is the object of “piglia,” and “sonno piglia” personifies sleep as something that seizes a man.
segments.inferno-3-g86: “Allor” sets the time as “then,” following Virgil’s previous reply. “Con li occhi vergognosi e bassi” is a prepositional phrase; “li” is the archaic masculine plural article for modern Italian “gli,” and the adjectives describe Dante’s eyes as ashamed and lowered.
segments.inferno-3-g87: “Temendo” is a gerund, giving the reason for Dante’s silence: “fearing that.” The phrase “no ’l mio dir li fosse grave” uses “fosse,” imperfect subjunctive, after a verb of fearing; “’l mio dir” is a substantivized infinitive meaning “my speaking,” and “li” means “to him,” though that sense is not in the supplied list.
segments.inferno-3-g88: “Infino al fiume” means “as far as/until the river.” “Del parlar mi trassi” is poetic word order: literally “I drew myself from speaking,” with “mi” reflexive and “trassi” a passato remoto form of “trarre,” here meaning “withdrew/refrained.”
segments.inferno-3-g89: “Ed ecco” introduces a sudden sight: “and behold.” The construction places the infinitive “venir” before its subject “un vecchio,” so the sense is “behold an old man coming toward us by boat/in a boat.”
segments.inferno-3-g90: “Bianco” agrees with “un vecchio” and means “white-haired” in this context. “Per antico pelo” gives the cause or means of his whiteness: because of, or by way of, his aged hair.
segments.inferno-3-g91: “Gridando” is a gerund modifying the old man: he is coming while shouting. It introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-3-g92: “Guai a voi” is an exclamatory formula: “woe to you.” The preposition “a” marks the person against whom the woe is directed.
segments.inferno-3-g93: “Anime prave” is a direct address to the souls being ferried. The adjective “prave,” feminine plural agreeing with “anime,” means morally wicked or perverse.
segments.inferno-3-g94: “Non isperate mai” is a negative command in the second-person plural: literally “do not ever hope,” hence “never hope.” “Veder lo cielo” uses the infinitive after “isperate”; “lo cielo” is archaic/article usage for “il cielo,” here meaning Heaven rather than merely the sky.
segments.inferno-3-g95: “I’” is an apocopated form of “io,” “I,” though the supplied lemma treats it as an article. “Vegno” is an archaic form of “vengo,” “I come,” and “per menarvi” expresses purpose: “in order to lead you”; “a l’altra riva” means “to the other bank,” and “ne le tenebre etterne” is archaic spelling for “nelle tenebre eterne,” “into/in the eternal darkness.”
segments.inferno-3-g96: “In caldo e ’n gelo” continues the destination or condition into which the souls are led. “’n” is a contracted poetic form of “in,” and the paired nouns “caldo” and “gelo” evoke the punishments of heat and icy cold.
segments.inferno-3-g97: “E tu” turns from the damned souls to Dante directly. “Che se’ costì” is a relative clause, with “se’” as the second-person singular of “essere” and “costì” meaning “there where you are,” near the addressee.
segments.inferno-3-g98: “Anima viva” is an appositive identifying Dante as a living soul. “Viva” is feminine singular to agree with “anima,” even though the person addressed is male.
segments.inferno-3-g99: “Pàrtiti” is an imperative with attached reflexive pronoun: “depart/separate yourself.” “Da cotesti che son morti” means “from those there near you who are dead,” with “che son morti” as a relative clause modifying “cotesti.”
segments.inferno-3-g100: “Ma poi che” functions as a temporal conjunction, “but after/when.” “Vide” is a simple past form, while “ch’io non mi partiva” uses the imperfect “partiva” with the reflexive clitic “mi,” meaning Dante was not departing or was not moving away.
segments.inferno-3-g101: “Disse” is the simple past of “dire,” introducing Charon’s direct speech. The subject is the same as in the previous segment: Charon, who has just observed Dante’s refusal to leave.
segments.inferno-3-g102: “Per altra via” means “by/through another way”; “per” marks the route or means by which Dante will travel. The adjective “altra” precedes “via” in a normal poetic order and contrasts this route with the present crossing-place.
segments.inferno-3-g103: “Per altri porti” continues the idea of a different route, literally “through/by other ports.” “Verrai” is future second-person singular, and “a piaggia” means “to the shore,” with “piaggia” here referring to a landing-place rather than a hillside.
segments.inferno-3-g104: “Non qui” is an elliptical correction: Dante will come to a shore, but “not here.” The brevity heightens Charon’s refusal of the present passage.
segments.inferno-3-g105: “Per passare” gives the purpose: “in order to cross.” The infinitive “passare” here means crossing the river, not merely passing by.
segments.inferno-3-g106: “Più lieve legno” is a poetic metonymy: “legno,” literally “wood,” means a boat or vessel, and “più lieve” means “lighter.” “Convien che ti porti” is an impersonal necessity construction, “it is necessary/fitting that it carry you,” with “porti” as a present subjunctive after “convien che.”
segments.inferno-3-g107: “E ’l duca lui” is elliptical: “and the guide [said] to him.” “’l” is the contracted article “il,” and “lui” is used as the object of the implied speaking, referring to Charon.
segments.inferno-3-g108: “Caron” is a vocative form addressing Charon directly. Dante often uses shortened or archaic forms for metrical and poetic effect.
segments.inferno-3-g109: “Non ti crucciare” is a negative command formed with “non” plus the infinitive, as in Italian negative imperatives. The reflexive “ti” makes the verb mean “do not upset yourself” or “do not be angry.”
segments.inferno-3-g110: “Vuolsi” is an archaic enclitic form meaning “it is willed,” with “si” attached to the verb. “Colà dove si puote ciò che si vuole” means “there where what is willed can be done”; the repeated impersonal “si” points to divine authority, not to an ordinary human subject.
segments.inferno-3-g111: “E più non dimandare” is another negative command: “and ask no more.” “Più non” has the sense “no longer” or “any more,” and “dimandare” is an older spelling/variant of “domandare.”
segments.inferno-3-g112: “Quinci” means “thereupon/from this,” referring to the effect of Virgil’s words. “Fuor quete” is a poetic form for “furono quete,” “were quieted,” and “le gote al nocchier” uses “al” as a possessive dative: literally “the cheeks to the boatman,” meaning “the boatman’s cheeks.”
segments.inferno-3-g113: “Che” introduces a relative clause describing the boatman, Charon. “’Ntorno a li occhi” means “around the eyes,” with elision in “’ntorno,” and “avea” is an archaic imperfect form of “aveva”; “di fiamme rote” means “circles/wheels of flame.”
segments.inferno-3-g114: “Ma” marks a contrast with Charon’s now-quieted appearance. “Quell’ anime” is an elided form of “quelle anime,” “those souls,” the plural feminine subject of the sentence.
segments.inferno-3-g115: “Ch’eran” contracts “che erano,” introducing a relative clause describing the souls. “Lasse e nude” are feminine plural adjectives agreeing with “anime,” meaning weary and naked.
segments.inferno-3-g116: “Cangiar” is a poetic shortened finite form equivalent to “cangiarono,” coordinated with “dibattero,” both simple past plural verbs. “Cangiar colore” means they turned pale or changed complexion, while “dibattero i denti” literally says they beat their teeth, i.e. their teeth chattered.
segments.inferno-3-g117: “Ratto che” is an idiom meaning “as soon as,” with “ratto” used adverbially. “’Nteser” is a shortened form of “intesero,” here meaning “heard,” and “le parole crude” refers to Charon’s harsh words.
segments.inferno-3-g56: The object phrase “Fama di loro” is fronted for emphasis: literally, “fame of them.” The subject is “il mondo,” and “esser” is an infinitive dependent on “lassa,” an archaic/poetic form for “lascia,” meaning “allows”; the order is “the world does not allow there to be fame of them.”
segments.inferno-3-g57: “Misericordia e giustizia” are coordinated abstract subjects, here best understood as divine Mercy and Justice. “Li” is an object pronoun meaning “them,” and the singular “sdegna” can be read poetically with the compound abstract subject as a unified judgment: mercy and justice reject them.
segments.inferno-3-g58: “Ragioniam” is the shortened first-person plural present form of “ragioniamo,” used imperatively: “let us talk/discuss.” “Lor” is an apocopated form of “loro,” governed by “di”: “of/about them.”
segments.inferno-3-g59: “Guarda” and “passa” are second-person singular imperatives addressed to Dante. The phrase contrasts with the previous command not to discuss them: instead, he should look and move on.
segments.inferno-3-g60: “E io” begins a new narrative movement and sets up the subject “I,” which is completed later by the main verb “vidi.” The conjunction “E” simply continues the account.
segments.inferno-3-g61: “Che riguardai” is a relative clause modifying “io”: “I, who looked.” “Riguardai” is passato remoto, used for a completed narrative action, and here means that Dante looked attentively or looked again.
segments.inferno-3-g62: “Vidi” is the main verb, passato remoto: “I saw.” “’Nsegna” is an elided/aferetic form of “insegna,” meaning “banner”; the relative clause “che girando correva tanto ratta” describes it as running while turning, with “tanto” preparing the result clause that follows.
segments.inferno-3-g63: This “che” is consecutive, dependent on the earlier “tanto”: “so swiftly that.” “Mi parea” is archaic for “mi pareva,” “it seemed to me,” and “d’ogne posa” means “of/from every rest,” i.e. unfit or unworthy of any pause.
segments.inferno-3-g64: “Dietro le” means “behind it,” with “le” referring back to the feminine noun “’nsegna.” “Venìa” is a poetic imperfect form of “veniva,” and the subject follows the verb: “sì lunga tratta di gente,” “so long a train of people,” which sets up the following result clause.
segments.inferno-3-g65: “Ch’i’” is contracted from “che io,” introducing the result of “sì lunga”: “that I would not have believed.” “Averei” is an archaic conditional form of “avrei,” auxiliary with “creduto”; “avesse disfatta” is pluperfect subjunctive in the dependent belief clause, with “n’” meaning “of them” and “tanta” implying “so many people.”
segments.inferno-3-g66: “Poscia ch’” means “after that/when,” introducing a temporal subordinate clause. “V’” is “vi,” meaning “there,” and “ebbi ... riconosciuto” is an anterior past construction: “I had recognized”; “alcun” means some person or a certain one.
segments.inferno-3-g67: “Vidi e conobbi” are both passato remoto forms, giving completed narrative actions: “I saw and recognized.” “Colui che” means “he who,” and “fece ... il gran rifiuto” literally means “made the great refusal”; “per viltade” gives the cause, “through/out of cowardice,” with “viltade” an archaic form.
segments.inferno-3-g68: “Incontanente” is an archaic adverb meaning “immediately.” “Intesi” is passato remoto, “I understood,” and “certo fui” places the predicate adjective before the verb for poetic emphasis: “I was certain”; “che” introduces the content of what Dante understood, and “d’i” is an old contraction for “dei,” “of the.”
segments.inferno-3-g69: This adjectival phrase modifies “i cattivi”: they are “spiacenti,” displeasing, both “a Dio” and “a’ nemici sui.” “A’” is a contraction of “ai,” and “sui” here is the possessive “suoi,” meaning “his,” referring to God: even God’s enemies find them displeasing.
segments.inferno-3-g131: “Così” links this action to the preceding comparison: just as leaves fall one by one, so the souls depart. “Sen vanno” is an archaic contraction of “se ne vanno,” with “ne” giving the sense of going away; “su per l’onda bruna” means over/across the dark water, with poetic word order but ordinary present tense.
segments.inferno-3-g132: “Avanti che” means “before,” and it takes the subjunctive “sien,” an archaic form of “siano.” “Discese” is a feminine plural past participle with “essere,” referring to the souls once they have gone down or landed “di là,” on the other side.
segments.inferno-3-g133: “Di qua” contrasts with “di là”: this side of the river versus the far side. “S’auna” is “si auna,” a reflexive/middle form meaning “gathers itself together”; the subject follows the verb, “nuova schiera,” a new crowd or troop.
segments.inferno-3-g134: “Figliuol” is a contracted poetic form of “figliuolo,” meaning “son” or “child.” With “mio,” it is a direct form of address, showing Virgil’s affectionate, guiding tone toward Dante.
segments.inferno-3-g135: “Disse” is the passato remoto of “dire,” the normal narrative past tense in Dante. “’l” is an apocopated form of “il,” and “maestro cortese” identifies Virgil as the courteous or noble guide.
segments.inferno-3-g136: “Quelli che” means “those who,” introducing the class of souls being described. “Muoion” is a poetic shortened form of “muoiono,” and “convegnon” is likewise a shortened present plural meaning “come together” or “assemble”; “d’ogne paese” means “from every land.”
segments.inferno-3-g137: The adjective “pronti” is placed before “sono” for emphasis: they are ready or eager. “A trapassar” is an infinitive construction after “pronti,” and “lo rio” uses the older article “lo” before “rio,” here meaning the river or stream to be crossed.
segments.inferno-3-g138: “Ché” introduces the reason: they are ready because divine justice urges them on. “Li” is a direct-object pronoun meaning “them,” referring to the damned souls, and “sprona” literally evokes spurring a horse forward.
segments.inferno-3-g139: “Sì che” forms a result clause: “so that.” “Si volve” is a reflexive or middle construction meaning “turns itself” or “is turned,” and “in disio” means “into desire,” with the archaic poetic noun “disio” for “desire.”
segments.inferno-3-g140: “Quinci” means “from here” or “by this way,” referring to the infernal crossing. “Non ... mai” is the standard negative pairing “not ever,” hence “never,” and “anima buona” is the subject placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.inferno-3-g141: “E però” means “and therefore,” drawing a conclusion from the fact that no good soul crosses here. It prepares the conditional explanation about Charon’s objection to Dante.
segments.inferno-3-g142: “Se” here means “if,” introducing the condition. “Di te” means “about you” or “concerning you,” and “si lagna” is a pronominal verb, “complains” or “laments,” with Charon as the subject.
segments.inferno-3-g143: “Ben puoi sapere” means “you can well know,” with “ben” strengthening the certainty. “Omai” is an older form of “ormai,” “by now,” and “che ’l suo dir suona” literally means “what his saying sounds,” idiomatically “what his words mean.”
segments.inferno-3-g144: “Finito questo” is an absolute participial phrase: literally “this finished,” meaning “when this was finished.” “Finito” is a past participle, and “questo” refers back to Virgil’s explanation.
segments.inferno-3-g145: “La buia campagna” is the subject, meaning the dark plain or region. “Tremò” is passato remoto, a completed narrative action, and “sì forte” means “so strongly/violently,” setting up the result clause that follows.
segments.inferno-3-g146: “Che” introduces the result of the violent trembling. “De lo” is the older separated form of modern “dello,” and “la mente ... mi bagna” personifies memory as still wetting Dante with sweat; “di sudore” expresses what he is wet with.
segments.inferno-3-g70: “Questi sciaurati” is the subject of the sentence; the demonstrative “Questi” points to the souls just identified in the preceding context. “Sciaurati” is an adjective used substantively, meaning “wretched ones” or “accursed wretches.”
segments.inferno-3-g71: “Che” introduces a relative clause modifying “sciaurati.” “Mai non” is the old Italian negative construction, where “mai” and “non” together mean “never”; “fur” is an apocopated poetic form of “furono,” the passato remoto of “essere.”
segments.inferno-3-g72: “Erano” is the main imperfect verb, describing the continuing condition of these souls. “Stimolati” is a passive participle coordinated with “ignudi,” and the two “da” phrases mark the agents of the torment; “ch’eran ivi” is a relative clause meaning “that were there.”
segments.inferno-3-g73: “Elle” refers back to the flies and wasps and is the subject of “rigavan,” an apocopated imperfect form of “rigavano.” “Lor” functions possessively or as a dative of possession, so “lor ... il volto” means “their face/faces”; “di sangue” expresses what the faces are streaked with.
segments.inferno-3-g74: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring most naturally to “sangue,” the blood just mentioned. It introduces the clause explaining what happens to that blood after it runs down.
segments.inferno-3-g75: “Mischiato” is a masculine singular past participle agreeing with the implied antecedent “sangue.” “Di lagrime” means “with tears” in sense, though the preposition is literally “of” in form.
segments.inferno-3-g76: The phrase begins with the place expression “a’ lor piedi,” with “a’” contracted from “a i,” meaning “at their feet.” “Era ricolto” is a passive imperfect construction, and “da fastidiosi vermi” gives the agent: the blood mixed with tears was being gathered up by repulsive worms.
segments.inferno-3-g77: “Poi ch’” forms the temporal conjunction “after” or “once.” “Mi diedi a riguardar” is literally “I gave myself to looking,” an idiom meaning “I set myself to look”; “oltre” adds the idea of looking farther ahead or beyond.
segments.inferno-3-g78: “Vidi” is passato remoto, the main narrative verb: “I saw.” “Genti” is plural in form and means “people” or “groups of people,” and “a la riva d’un gran fiume” gives their location, “at/on the bank of a great river.”
segments.inferno-3-g79: “Per ch’” is “per che,” literally “for which,” functioning as “wherefore” or “for this reason.” “Io dissi” is a straightforward passato remoto reporting Dante’s speech.
segments.inferno-3-g80: “Maestro” is a vocative addressed to Virgil. The title marks him as Dante’s guide and master rather than merely as a schoolteacher.
segments.inferno-3-g81: “Or” means “now,” introducing Dante’s request, and “concedi” is an imperative-like present form: “grant.” The clause “ch’i’ sappia” contains the subjunctive “sappia” after the request, and “quali sono” asks who or what sort they are.
segments.inferno-3-g82: “E qual costume” continues the indirect question begun after “ch’i’ sappia.” “Le” refers back to “genti,” and “fa ... parer” is a causative construction, “makes them seem”; “di trapassar” depends on “pronte,” meaning ready or eager to cross over.
segments.inferno-3-g83: “Com’” is “come,” here meaning “as,” and “i’” is the poetic shortened form of “io.” “Discerno” is present tense, and “per lo fioco lume” means that Dante can make this out only through or by means of the dim light.
segments.inferno-3-g84: “Ed elli a me” is an elliptical speech formula: literally “and he to me,” with a verb such as “said” understood. “Elli” is an archaic form of “egli,” meaning “he,” though that sense is not represented in the supplied sense list.
segments.inferno-3-g85: “Le cose ti fier conte” is archaic: “fier” is an old future form of “essere,” and “conte” here means “known/clear,” so the phrase means “the things will be known to you.” “Fermerem” is an apocopated future form, “fermeremo,” and “li nostri passi” is the direct object, “our steps”; “su la trista riviera d’Acheronte” gives the place where they will stop.
segments.inferno-3-g118: “Bestemmiavano” is imperfect indicative, describing an ongoing or repeated action: they were cursing/blaspheming. The objects follow directly: “Dio” and “lor parenti,” where archaic/poetic “lor” means “their.”
segments.inferno-3-g119: This segment continues the list of things cursed, with repeated “e” piling up the objects: the human species, the place, the time, and the seed/origin. “’l” is the elided article “il,” and “lor” again means “their”; “di lor semenza e di lor nascimenti” depends on “seme,” specifying the source of their engendering and birth.
segments.inferno-3-g120: “Poi” marks the next action in the narrative. “Si ritrasser” is an archaic apocopated form of “si ritrassero,” passato remoto/reflexive: they drew themselves back or withdrew; “tutte quante insieme” emphatically means all of them together.
segments.inferno-3-g121: “Piangendo” is a gerund, giving the manner in which they withdrew: while weeping. “Forte” functions adverbially here, meaning loudly or intensely, not simply “strong.”
segments.inferno-3-g122: “A la” is the older separated form of modern “alla,” meaning “to the.” The relative clause “ch’attende...” modifies “riva malvagia”: the shore awaits “ciascun uom,” every man; the final “che Dio non teme” is another relative clause, “who does not fear/revere God.”
segments.inferno-3-g123: “Caron dimonio” is the subject phrase, with “dimonio” in apposition: Charon, the demon. The shortened “Caron” is a poetic form of “Caronte.”
segments.inferno-3-g124: “Con occhi di bragia” is an instrumental/descriptive phrase: with eyes like burning embers. “Loro accennando” uses the gerund “accennando” with “loro” as an indirect object, meaning that he is beckoning to them.
segments.inferno-3-g125: “Raccoglie” is present indicative, giving a vivid narrative present: he gathers. “Le” is a feminine plural direct-object pronoun referring back to the souls, and “tutte” emphasizes that he gathers all of them.
segments.inferno-3-g126: “Batte” is another vivid present tense: he strikes. “Col” contracts “con il,” while “qualunque s’adagia” means whoever settles down, lingers, or delays; “s’” is the reflexive pronoun elided before the vowel.
segments.inferno-3-g127: “Come” introduces the comparison: as leaves do in autumn. “Si levan” is an archaic/poetic present form with apocopated “levano,” literally “remove themselves” or “come off”; “l’una appresso de l’altra” means one after another, with “de” for “di.”
segments.inferno-3-g128: “Fin che” functions as the conjunction “until.” The branch is personified as seeing its leaves on the ground; “a la terra” is the older separated form of modern “alla terra,” here meaning on/at the earth, and “spoglie” metaphorically calls the fallen leaves its cast-off coverings.
segments.inferno-3-g129: “Similemente” brings in the main comparison: just so the souls act like the autumn leaves. “Il mal seme d’Adamo” is a collective phrase for sinful humanity, while “gittansi” is poetic for “si gittano,” with the reflexive pronoun attached after the verb; “ad una ad una” is the idiom “one by one.”
segments.inferno-3-g130: “Per cenni” gives the means or prompt: by signs or gestures. The final simile “come augel per suo richiamo” compares the souls to a bird responding to its lure-call; “augel” is a poetic form for “uccello.”

sentences.inferno-3-s1: Through me one goes into the sorrowful city, through me one goes into eternal pain, through me one goes among the lost people.
sentences.inferno-3-s2: Justice moved my lofty Maker; divine power made me, supreme wisdom and primal love.
sentences.inferno-3-s3: Before me nothing was created except eternal things, and I endure eternally.
sentences.inferno-3-s4: Abandon every hope, you who enter.
sentences.inferno-3-s5: I saw these words of dark color written at the top of a gate; whereupon I said, “Master, their meaning is hard for me.”
sentences.inferno-3-s6: And he said to me, like a prudent person: “Here one must leave behind every fear; every cowardice must here be dead.”
sentences.inferno-3-s7: We have come to the place where I told you that you would see the sorrowful people who have lost the good of the intellect.”
sentences.inferno-3-s8: And after he had placed his hand in mine with a cheerful face, whereat I took heart, he brought me inside to the hidden things.
sentences.inferno-3-s17: And I: “Master, what is so burdensome to them that it makes them lament so loudly?”
sentences.inferno-3-s19: He replied: “I shall tell it to you very briefly.”
sentences.inferno-3-s20: These have no hope of death, and their blind life is so low that they are envious of every other fate.
sentences.inferno-3-s14: And he said to me: “This wretched condition is borne by the sorrowful souls of those who lived without infamy and without praise.”
sentences.inferno-3-s15: They are mingled with that wretched choir of the angels who were not rebellious, nor were they faithful to God, but were for themselves.
sentences.inferno-3-s16: The heavens drive them out so as not to be less beautiful, nor does deep Hell receive them, because the wicked would have some glory from them.”
sentences.inferno-3-s9: There sighs, weeping, and loud wails resounded through the starless air, so that at the beginning I wept because of them.
sentences.inferno-3-s10: Various tongues, horrible speech, words of pain, accents of anger, voices loud and faint, and the sound of hands with them made an uproar, which forever whirls in that timeless, stained air, like sand when a whirlwind blows.
sentences.inferno-3-s11: And I, whose head was encircled by bewilderment, said: “Master, what is that which I hear?
sentences.inferno-3-s12: and what people is this that seems so overcome in grief?”
sentences.inferno-3-s45: The tearful earth gave forth a wind, whereupon a crimson light flashed, which overcame every sense in me; and I fell like a man whom sleep seizes.
sentences.inferno-3-s29: Then, with ashamed and downcast eyes, fearing that my speech might be burdensome to him, I kept myself from speaking until we reached the river.
sentences.inferno-3-s30: And behold, coming toward us in a boat, an old man, white-haired with ancient hair, shouting: “Woe to you, wicked souls!”
sentences.inferno-3-s31: Never hope to see Heaven: I come to lead you to the other bank, into the eternal darkness, into heat and frost.
sentences.inferno-3-s32: And you who are there, living soul, separate yourself from these who are dead.”
sentences.inferno-3-s33: But when he saw that I was not leaving, he said: “By another way, by other ports, you will come to the shore to cross, not here: a lighter vessel must carry you.”
sentences.inferno-3-s34: And my guide said to him: “Charon, do not be angry: it is willed so there where what is willed can be done, and ask no more.”
sentences.inferno-3-s35: Thereupon the woolly cheeks of the boatman of the livid marsh grew quiet, he who had circles of flame around his eyes.
sentences.inferno-3-s36: But those souls, who were weary and naked, changed color and chattered their teeth as soon as they heard the cruel words.
sentences.inferno-3-s21: The world does not allow fame of them to exist; mercy and justice disdain them: let us not speak of them, but look and pass on.
sentences.inferno-3-s22: And I, who looked, saw a banner that, whirling, ran so swiftly that it seemed to me unworthy of any rest; and behind it came so long a train of people that I would not have believed death had undone so many.
sentences.inferno-3-s23: After I had recognized someone there, I saw and recognized the shade of him who, out of cowardice, made the great refusal.
sentences.inferno-3-s24: At once I understood and was certain that this was the sect of the wretched, displeasing to God and to his enemies.
sentences.inferno-3-s41: Thus they go away over the dark water, and before they have descended on the far side, on this side again a new troop gathers.
sentences.inferno-3-s42: “My son,” said the courteous master, “those who die in the wrath of God all assemble here from every country; and they are ready to cross the stream, because divine justice spurs them on, so that fear turns into desire.
sentences.inferno-3-s43: From here no good soul ever passes; and therefore, if Charon complains about you, you can now well know what his words mean.”
sentences.inferno-3-s44: When this was finished, the dark plain quaked so violently that the memory of the terror still bathes me in sweat.
sentences.inferno-3-s25: These wretches, who never were alive, were naked and greatly goaded by gadflies and by wasps that were there.
sentences.inferno-3-s26: They streaked their faces with blood, which, mixed with tears, was gathered at their feet by loathsome worms.
sentences.inferno-3-s27: And after I set myself to looking farther on, I saw people on the bank of a great river; wherefore I said: “Master, now grant me that I may know who they are, and what custom makes them appear so ready to cross over, as I discern through the faint light.”
sentences.inferno-3-s28: And he said to me: “The matter will be made clear to you when we halt our steps on the sorrowful bank of Acheron.”
sentences.inferno-3-s37: They cursed God and their parents, the human race, and the place and the time and the seed of their begetting and of their births.
sentences.inferno-3-s38: Then they all drew back together, weeping loudly, to the wicked shore that awaits every man who does not fear God.
sentences.inferno-3-s39: Charon the demon, with eyes of glowing coals, beckoning to them, gathers them all; he strikes with his oar whoever lingers.
sentences.inferno-3-s40: As in autumn the leaves fall away one after another, until the branch sees all its cast-off coverings on the ground, so likewise the evil seed of Adam cast themselves from that shore one by one, at his signs, like a bird at its lure-call.
